articles.title: Emotional reactions to climate change predict climate-friendly outcomes in 63 cross-country samples
articles.abstract: As the predicted impacts of climate change on nature and society worsen, it is essential to not only understand how to communicate climate risks but also how communicating them affects people’s climate-relevant emotions, scientific beliefs, political attitudes, and behavior around the world. Here, we conducted a preregistered secondary analysis of the “negative emotional induction” condition of the International Climate Psychology Collaboration (Doell, Todorova, Vlasceanu, et al., 2024). Participants (N = 5,167) from 63 cross-country samples reported eight emotional reactions–anger, anxiety, depression, fear, guilt, helplessness, hope, indifference–to climate change before and after receiving an emotionally salient, negatively valenced, text about the impacts of climate change. Participants’ belief in climate change, support for nine climate mitigation policies, willingness to share a climate post on social media, and online climate-friendly behavior were assessed. We found that anger, anxiety, depression, fear, and guilt increased post-intervention, and an aggregate score of these negative emotions in turn predicted an increase in all climate-friendly outcomes across all cross-country samples. Conversely, optimistic indifference–hope and indifference–decreased and predicted a decrease in climate beliefs and behavior. This negative effect on climate-friendly outcomes was primarily predicted by feeling indifferent to climate change, rather than helpless. Together, these results show that the interplay between multiple emotional reactions to climate change is an important predictor of climate-friendly outcomes across 63 cross-country samples, and that climate communications should focus on reducing indifference to the climate crisis.

articles.title: Job Quality, Work–Family Conflict, Anxiety, and Family Well-Being Among Young Workers in Singapore
articles.authors: Cameron Tan, Irene YH Ng, Xi Wen Chan, Gerard Chung
articles.abstract: Objective: To examine associations between job quality, work–family conflict (WFC), generalised anxiety disorder (GAD), and family well-being (FWB) among young employees in Singapore. Background: Work-related demands and resources may spill over across life domains, shaping individual strain and family functioning. Early adulthood involves overlapping career and family transitions that may heighten vulnerability to WFC. However, young workers remain underexamined, and much of the existing evidence derives from Western contexts. Methods: Data were drawn from Wave 3 of the In-Work Poverty study, comprising 677 full-time employees aged 21–35. Multidimensional job quality was measured using an adaptation of Eurofound’s Job Quality Index. Regression and path analyses examined associations between job quality, WFC, GAD, and FWB, adjusting for sociodemographic and occupational characteristics. Results: Higher job quality was associated with lower levels of WFC and GAD, with progressively better outcomes observed from strained to highly resourced jobs. FWB was also higher in more resourced job categories, although associations were weaker and less consistent. WFC partially mediated the association between job quality and GAD, but not FWB. Conclusion: Multidimensional job quality was associated with mental health and family outcomes among young workers in a non-Western context. While WFC helps account for associations with GAD, the more complex pattern for FWB suggests that family outcomes may depend on additional relational and household processes beyond individual role strain.

articles.title: Urban Science Beyond Samples: Up-to-Date Street Network Models and Indicators for Every Urban Area in the World
articles.abstract: Urban planners need up-to-date, global, and consistent street network models and indicators to measure resilience and performance, model accessibility, and target local quality-of-life interventions. This article presents up-to-date street network models and indicators for every urban area in the world. It uses 2025 urban area boundaries from the Global Human Settlement Layer, allowing users to join these data to hundreds of other urban attributes. Its workflow ingests 180 million OpenStreetMap nodes and 360 million OpenStreetMap edges across 10,351 urban areas in 189 countries. The code, models, and indicators are publicly available for reuse. These resources unlock worldwide urban street network science beyond samples as well as local analyses in under-resourced regions where models and indicators are otherwise less-accessible.

articles.title: Instruments and Artefacts: Mapping the Adoption of Large Language Models in Social Sciences
articles.authors: Bogdan Mamaev, Qizhou Wang, Matteo Vergani, Christopher Leckie
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly reshaping social science research. This study analyses a corpus of 12,223 papers (2019–2026) to identify methodological shifts and disciplinary divergences. Using a hybrid approach combining LLM-based quantitative surveys with BERTopic thematic analysis, we trace how these models are employed across social science fields. Our findings reveal exponential growth in LLM adoption, dominated by proprietary models - primarily OpenAI’s GPT family via commercial APIs. We establish a divide between tool-oriented disciplines, which utilise LLMs for data extraction and prediction, and reflective-critical disciplines, which interrogate models as cultural artifacts shaped by bias. We document a transition from simple text classification to advanced applications in simulation, text-based modelling, and synthetic data generation. However, these developments have outpaced rigorous evaluative frameworks, creating a "validation gap." Reliance on black-box LLMs poses challenges for reproducibility, transparency, and measurement stability. We conclude that for the social sciences to successfully integrate LLMs, the field must move beyond reliance on commercial providers and prioritise domain-specific standards for validation and reporting, and adopt more open alternatives.

articles.title: Friends on the Other Side: Receptiveness to Opposing Views Predicts Formation of Politically Heterogeneous Relationships
articles.authors: Brian Philip Reschke, Julia Minson, Hannah Riley Bowles, Mathijs de Vaan, Sameer Srivastava
articles.abstract: Growing evidence points to the corrosive influence of partisanship on society. We examine the relationship between “receptiveness to opposing views” (i.e., an individual difference in the willingness to engage with information from opposing perspectives) and the propensity to form positive and close collaborative relationships with ideologically opposed others. Across three studies varying in sampling and methodological approaches—a retrospective network study, a time-lagged field study, and an experiment—we find that individual and mutual receptiveness mitigate the negative influence of ideological opposition on relationship formation and willingness to collaborate. We find evidence that receptiveness is distinctively influential as compared to related personality characteristics (e.g., extraversion, self-monitoring, agreeableness, openness, intellectual humility). These findings contribute to our understanding of how individual differences shape social network formation and collaboration across ideological divides.

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence, Value-Based Care, and Episode-Based Payment Reform in the United States: A Policy Case for Accountable Adoption
articles.abstract: Value-based care remains one of the clearest payment and delivery reforms available to the United States healthcare system. It pays for prevention, coordination, quality, and responsible use of resources rather than volume alone. Artificial intelligence can support that shift when organizations use it to identify actionable risk, make episodes of care easier to understand, improve navigation, reduce avoidable administrative work, and connect clinical decisions to measurable outcomes (Topol, 2019). This policy review supports broader use of value-based care, bundled payments, and episode-based analytics, while arguing that adoption needs careful governance. The main point is simple: artificial intelligence should not be treated as a stand-alone technology purchase. It should be used as infrastructure for accountable care. The paper links data infrastructure, risk prediction, bundled payment design, episode definitions, provider performance measurement, care coordination, economic evaluation, equity monitoring, and institutional accountability. Episode-based payment and bundled payment models are especially useful because they show the full course of care, encourage coordination across settings, and give artificial intelligence tools a practical target: forecasting cost, finding quality gaps, and prompting timely intervention.

articles.title: Cross-Disciplinary Lessons for Improving Informed Consent Procedures in Research
articles.authors: Derek Robey, Hannah Baron, Sebastian Karcher, Dessislava Kirilova, Justine Davis, John Baumann, Rebecca Campbell, Scott Desposato, Roma Dhamanaskar, James DuBois
articles.abstract: Informed consent shouldn’t be reduced to a checkbox. Treating consent as an ongoing, participant‑centered process of engagement that is clearer, tailored to research context, and transparent about data, can strengthen ethical research and trust across both biomedical and social science disciplines, as they learn from each other.

articles.title: Reconfiguring Teacher Agency in the Age of Generative AI: A Quasi-Experimental Study of the Relational Turn in Instructional Ecologies
articles.authors: XI ZHAN, Yuxiang Shi, Li Ma
articles.abstract: The rapid integration of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) into lesson design and instruction raises questions about how teachers’ instructional agency is reconfigured. This STEAM class-based quasi-experimental study involved 36 teachers and 462 students across three conditions: traditional teacher community, GAI-mediated group, and relational group. Grounded in Ihde’s (1990) human–technology relations theory, we combined quantitative analyses of student test performance and water-purification outcomes with video-based Lag Sequential Analysis of instructional behaviors. While student assessments showed no significant between-group differences, treating GAI as a partner rather than a tool reshaped teacher collaboration and classroom dynamics. Our findings suggest that human-GAI co-agency facilitates teaching and learning, and the impact of GAI is deeply embedded in the instructional ecology. A relational–ecological model of human–AI instructional agency and practical implications are discussed.

articles.title: Mary Pearcey's Final Message in the Spanish-Language Press, 1890–1891: From Madrid to Manila
articles.abstract: This research note presents the first systematic documentation of Spanish-language press coverage of the Mary Pearcey execution case across at least thirteen distinct newspapers spanning Madrid, Barcelona, and Manila, published between 23 December 1890 and 12 February 1891. Located primarily in the Hemeroteca Digital of the Biblioteca Nacional de España, these sources reveal that the Pearcey execution story was transmitted from London to Spain within hours via the London–Bilbao submarine telegraph cable, distributed by the Agencia Fabra (Spanish correspondent of the French Havas news agency), and published simultaneously on 23 December 1890 — the very day of execution — across at least nine Madrid daily newspapers spanning the full political spectrum: El Demócrata, El Diario Español, La Época, El Estandarte, La Iberia, La Opinión, El Siglo Futuro, El Siglo, and La Unión Católica. Two additional Madrid newspapers — El País and La República — published the dispatch the following day. La Vanguardia of Barcelona carried coverage on 28 December 1890. Most remarkably, the dispatch reached the Spanish colonial press, appearing in El Comercio (Manila) on 26 January 1891 and El Resumen (Manila) on 12 February 1891 — demonstrating that a Victorian British murder case traveled from London across three continents to the Philippines in approximately five weeks. All newspapers report Pearcey's final message: «No te he hecho traición» ("I have not betrayed you"). The Spanish translation's addition of the personal pronoun "te" makes the implied recipient explicit, absent from English-language versions. Analysis reveals two distinct telegraph dispatches in circulation, with five newspapers printing both dispatches on separate pages of the same edition. These findings contribute to Victorian crime historiography, transnational media history, and the study of nineteenth-century international news infrastructure. How to Cite This Paper Jacobs, T., & Donaldson, A. V. (2026). Mary Pearcey’s Final Message in the Spanish-Language Press, 1890–1891: From Madrid to Manila. SocArXiv. https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/z2wqs_v1

articles.title: Mirror or Membrane? Semantic Information, Collateral, and the Limits of Green Finance
articles.abstract: Sustainable finance is usually framed as an information problem: if markets receive better information about climate and biodiversity, prices and allocation will adjust. This paper asks a different question: whether such information becomes *semantic* for finance — causally consequential for a specified viability. Drawing on Rovelli and Kolchinsky–Wolpert, I distinguish two viability functions for wholesale finance: financial viability ($V_1$), defined by settlement, liquidity and collateral acceptability; and biophysically conditioned viability ($V_2$), defined by the long-run persistence of the finance–economy system under planetary constraints. I apply the diagnostic to the collateral complex, focusing on Eurosystem collateral eligibility and euro-area repo markets. The argument turns on a permutation criterion: if two instruments differ biophysically but agree in the parameters governing eligibility, haircutting, substitution, rehypothecation and liquidation, the complex treats them identically, and biophysical information can be scrambled at no operative cost. The collateral complex is semantically rich under $V_1$ and approaches semantic emptiness under $V_2$ on the biophysical dimension as an independent operative variable. Green labels and taxonomies increase syntactic information; absent operative changes they cannot repair this semantic misalignment — an architecture densely responsive to the conditions of its own reproduction and structurally insensitive to the conditions of planetary reproduction.

articles.title: Holding the Human: Conditions for Sustaining Human Centrality in Leadership Theory
articles.abstract: Across a structured analysis of twenty-five seminal contributions spanning five eras of leadership theory, a consistent structural pattern emerges. As the field expands the conceptual language of the human, it does not establish the structural capacity to sustain the human as an independent explanatory center. Instead, the human is progressively rendered variable, functionalized, and ultimately absorbed into relational and systemic formulations. This paper formalizes that pattern through a forced-choice coding analysis across seven analytical layers, demonstrates the trajectory through close engagement with canonical contributions, and advances a minimal theoretical response. We introduce the construct of Human Sustainment Capacity (HSC) and specify four necessary conditions (interpretive irreducibility, agency preservation under structure, bidirectional causality, and stability under load, the holding condition) that a leadership model must satisfy to prevent the observed transition from integration to absorption. The aim is not to propose another human-centered construct, but to define the structural requirements under which human agency, meaning, and judgment remain causally necessary in leadership explanation. We conclude with boundary conditions, an illustrative application to transformational leadership, and implications for theory and research. Keywords: human sustainment capacity; leadership theory; structural analysis; forced-choice coding; post-heroic leadership; holding condition; interpretive irreducibility; agency preservation; bidirectional causality; stability under load; transformational leadership; relational leadership; distributed leadership; theoretical contribution; meta-theory; human centrality; explanatory architecture

articles.title: Vocal Organ: Apparatus for Generating Voice and Images
articles.abstract: Drawing upon the experience of a congenitally deaf individual reconstructing phonation mechanisms, this study explores the human voice as a nexus where bodily materiality, technological media, and cognitive organization intersect. Employing a methodological path of mutual verification between artistic practice and interdisciplinary theory, the research reconstructs Peruvian Whistling Vessels, conducts a comparative analysis of historical speaking machines, and constructs a physical vocal tract model. Through these practices, it demonstrates that vocalization is not merely an acoustic output, but a dynamic process contingent upon whole-body coordination, anatomical constraints, and perceptual reorganization. The paper systematically traces the technological evolution from textual inscription and the phonograph to contemporary AI speech synthesis, illustrating how successive media iterations gradually detach the timbral materiality and bodily presence of sound, ultimately reducing the human voice to an infinitely replicable stream of algorithmic data. On cognitive and philosophical levels, the study re-examines the "Chinese Room" thought experiment and the phenomenon of blindsight, proposing that the emergence of intelligence does not rely on symbolic computation or pre-programmed algorithms, but is instead rooted in a cybernetic feedback loop of "articulation → internal monitoring → correction → re-articulation." Without a physical cavity capable of generating an informational "echo," a system cannot establish an ontological commitment, nor can it bridge the gap from symbolic processing to embodied understanding. This research argues that imperfect imitation and alternative sensory configurations are not passive compensations for physiological absence, but constitute a productive field capable of generating novel perceptual logics and creative methodologies. By defining artistic practice as a dynamic methodology that changes in real-time with the situation, this study provides an interdisciplinary framework for embodied cognition, media archaeology, and the philosophy of artificial intelligence within a post-human context. It further offers theoretical reference points for understanding perceptual repair, the reconstruction of vocal materiality, and the mechanisms of creative generation in the technological era. Keywords: Voice Materiality, Embodied Cognition, Ontological Commitment, Media Archaeology, Cybernetic Feedback, Artistic Research

articles.title: Epistemic Infrastructure Under Attack: Conceptualizing the Capture and Destruction of Public Knowledge Institutions
articles.abstract: From organized disinformation campaigns on social media to politically motivated attempts to take over publishers and platforms, the last decade has seen a proliferation of attacks on the epistemic infrastructure that people rely on to understand the world. We argue that scholars, practitioners, and policymakers have struggled to respond effectively to these threats in part because they lack systematic ways to understand the shifting nature of what is under threat and how it is being attacked. Drawing on Elinor Ostrom’s classic work on commons governance, we develop a conceptual framework that conceptualizes epistemic infrastructure as a shared resource system whose three interdependent components— knowledge, governance, and legitimacy—can each be attacked independently by motivated actors through two distinct modes: capture and destruction. Applied to recent attacks on Wikipedia, public broadcasters, and government data agencies, the framework reveals how attacks frequently target governance structures and institutional legitimacy rather than the knowledge outputs and information flows that existing approaches focus on, with implications for how scholars and practitioners diagnose and respond to these threats.

articles.title: The patterns of academic mobility and research collaboration in Latin America & Caribbean and Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.abstract: This study provides a comparative analysis of the international mobility, collaboration networks, disciplinary structures, and authorship roles of researchers from Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) and Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) from 1990 to 2023. Our findings show that both regions maintain long-standing ties with Europe and North America, but also exhibit a significant rise in intra-regional collaboration, particularly in SSA, where regional partnerships now exceed those with Europe and Central Asia. Engagement with East Asia and the Pacific has expanded as well, especially in SSA. Disciplinary patterns reveal persistent differences between SSH and STEM fields, with SSH maintaining higher shares of solo authorship but gradually increasing collaboration. Finally, authorship analysis indicates that although first-author roles decline in international collaborations, last-author positions have grown, especially in SSA, suggesting a gradual strengthening of senior research leadership. Together, these findings highlight evolving forms of scientific integration and capacity building across two key Global South regions.

articles.title: Building a Shock–Crisis Index: Development and Initial Validation of a Diagnostic Tool
articles.abstract: Management research still lacks a disciplined way to distinguish a severe external interruption from a deeper condition of organisational deterioration. Existing literature on shocks, crises, disruption, and resilience captures parts of that problem, but it often blurs trigger, condition, response, and outcome. This paper develops and initially validates the Shock–Crisis Index (SCI), a theory-led diagnostic architecture designed to distinguish bounded post-shock disruption from a more persistent, stock-eroding organisational condition. The SCI is built around three proposed condition-forming dimensions—temporal persistence, stock erosion, and adaptive capacity—and is implemented as a formative composite rather than a reflective latent scale. The empirical setting is a panel of 3,104 listed U.S. firms observed around the COVID-19 disruption, used here as a common adverse setting for initial validation rather than as a clean causal identification environment. The evidence indicates that the SCI generates meaningful dispersion, exhibits limited indicator redundancy, remains broadly stable across alternative weighting specifications, and aligns with independent fragility and recovery markers, including Altman Z-prime, distress probability, recovery duration, and composite distress signals. These findings provide initial support for a condition-based diagnostic approach to the shock–crisis boundary, while also clarifying that the present study is stronger on classificatory coherence than on full causal adjudication. The paper contributes by reframing crisis as a post-disturbance organisational condition with distinct dynamics, by offering a formative measurement architecture for diagnosing that condition, and by providing a first empirical validation that can be tested and refined across other exogenous shock settings.

articles.title: Institutional Corruption and Green Crime in Rural Ghana: A Social and Ecological Disorganisation Analysis of Gold Mining Under the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) Extractivist Regime
articles.abstract: This chapter examines the intersection of institutional corruption and environmental crime in rural Ghana, focusing on gold extraction. Using a social and ecological disorganisation framework, it argues that the erosion of governance structures, shaped by colonial legacies and enduring legal dualism, has enabled environmental and social harm. Weak regulatory enforcement, widespread corruption among statutory and customary authorities, and limited state capacity intensify social and ecological breakdown. Chinese companies, particularly those linked to the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), exploit these disordered contexts to carry out environmentally destructive practices with little accountability. The resulting degradation disproportionately affects rural populations as collusion between multinational actors and local elites further weakens resistance and undermine collective governance in rural Ghana. Structural constraints, including conflicting land rights, jurisdictional overlap, and fragmented enforcement, impede efforts to address rural green crime. The chapter concludes that without addressing the systemic roots of social disorganisation and corruption, green crime will persist, driven by global mineral demand, deepening the marginalisation of rural communities and accelerating ecological decline in rural Ghana.

articles.title: Reconciling Diverging Meanings of Just Transitions in a Fragmented World
articles.authors: Rudeena Jabar, Arohi Patil, Anjali Sharma, Juhi Chatterjee
articles.abstract: Just transition, an integral part of climate action, has become increasingly popular in both academic discussions and global climate negotiations. In 2022, the Just Transition Work Programme (JTWP) was established with the aim of discussing pathways to just transition in a climate-constrained world. However, the negotiations at JTWP have been slow due to fundamental disagreements in the interpretation of just transition between the Global North and Global South countries. The just transition (JT) scholarship has paid limited attention to explain the reasons for the starkly different understandings of JT across countries. In this paper, we first review academic literature on JT frameworks to understand whether and how they discuss the tensions that emerge when applying JT theories in practice. As we find limited academic literature on this topic, we develop a conceptual framework that focuses on highlighting and understanding the tensions that emerge in operationalizing academic JT frameworks in the real world. In particular, we focus on explaining tensions across different types of justice, geographical scales, and time. We then apply this framework to analyze JTWP negotiation proceedings. Our findings suggest that the existing justice frameworks do not consider the interactions between different types of justice, geographies, and time scales. This often leads them to ignore the tensions that could arise and the trade-offs that are involved in achieving a comprehensive vision of justice for all parts of the world at the same time. Through our framework, we are able to explore these tensions and explain the slow progress on JTWP negotiations. In the end, we provide examples to illustrate strategies that could be used to highlight and potentially resolve these tensions and, in turn, contribute to globally just low-carbon energy transitions.

articles.title: “You met me at a very chinese time in my life”: “China-maxxing” and the rise of intimate mediascapes
articles.abstract: “Chinamaxxing” is an Internet trend emerging in late 2025–early 2026, spanning ironic memes, conspiritual content, and aspirational lifestyle vlogs. Across these practices, global users construct an ambiguous project of “becoming Chinese.” We introduce the concept of “intimate mediascapes” to theorize how contemporary platform environments intensify affective engagement with images. Extending Appadurai’s notion of mediascapes, we argue that mediated images are no longer primarily encountered as representations of distant worlds, but as habitual, immersive resources that organize everyday life. In Chinamaxxing, what circulates is not an alternative “truth” about China, but a different mode of imagining through images. Drawing on dialogic auto-ethnography, we mobilize our divergent media habitus across distinct ecosystems. We show how algorithmic attention economies and multimodal user practices generate fleeting yet powerful forms of cultural intimacy, enabling a diffuse, vernacular form of soft power grounded in affect, routine, and everyday image-making rather than state-led cultural diplomacy.

articles.title: Strategic Management in the Digital Age: How Artificial Intelligence-Driven Digital Intelligence Systems Enable Comprehensive Crisis and Reputation Management
articles.abstract: In the contemporary digital landscape, organizations operate within increasingly complex, volatile, and interconnected environments where crises no longer emerge in isolated or linear patterns. Instead, they are dynamically constructed, amplified, and disseminated through digital ecosystems characterized by algorithmic mediation, real-time communication flows, and emotionally driven audience engagement. In this context, crisis and reputation management has evolved from a reactive communication function into a strategically embedded organizational capability that intersects with technology, data science, behavioral psychology, and strategic decision-making. This study explores the transformation of strategic management in the digital age through the integration of Artificial Intelligence and digital intelligence systems. It proposes a comprehensive framework for crisis and reputation management that shifts organizational practice from fragmented, reactive responses toward a holistic, continuous, and system-driven model. The study argues that traditional crisis communication theories are no longer sufficient to address the complexity of modern digital crises, which are shaped by multi-platform interactions, rapid information diffusion, and algorithmic amplification mechanisms. The research highlights that digital crises are increasingly influenced by a combination of technological systems and human behavioral dynamics. On one hand, digital platforms enable unprecedented speed in information dissemination, while on the other hand, they intensify emotional reactions, misinformation spread, and narrative polarization. These conditions necessitate the adoption of intelligent systems capable of real-time monitoring, data integration, sentiment analysis, and decision support. The study conceptualizes crisis and reputation management as an integrated system composed of five interconnected layers: data acquisition, analytical intelligence, decision support, communication execution, and post-crisis evaluation. Within this architecture, AI-driven systems function as the central enabling mechanism that connects data inputs with strategic organizational responses. These systems are not limited to automation but extend to cognitive augmentation, supporting human decision-makers in interpreting complex and rapidly evolving situations.

articles.title: Unintended Politics: Opinion Expression and Incivility in Incidental Political Discussion
articles.authors: Yibin Fan, Benjamin Mako Hill, Patricia Moy
articles.abstract: While substantial research has focused on how political talk can reinforce or undermine democratic norms, relatively little attention has been paid to the role of incidental political discussion in everyday, nonpolitical contexts. Embedded within contexts of shared, nonpartisan identities or preexisting social relationships, existing theory suggests that incidental political discussion will include less political opinion and uncivil expression than purposive political discussion. Analyses of nearly three thousand comments on mass shootings and gun control across both political and nonpolitical communities on Reddit show that, contrary to expectations, incidental political discussions are more likely than purposive political discussions to contain expressions of policy opinion. Additionally, the level of incivility does not differ between the two kinds of discussions.

articles.title: Do ICE Arrests Spark Political Action?
articles.authors: Hans Hassell, Taylor Hill, John B. Holbein
articles.abstract: Does U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity trigger electoral mobilization? Theory and prior evidence suggest it should: historical enforcement has driven citizens to the polls, contemporary enforcement has sparked widespread protests, and detention and deportation impose exceptionally high stakes on affected communities. The second Trump administration’s enforcement surge represents perhaps the most theoretically favorable conditions ever observed for detecting such a response. Using data from 2023 to 2025, we test whether recent ICE activity has translated into changes in voter registration and political donations using a nationwide county × day panel. Using Difference-in-differences, event study, and regression discontinuity in time designs, we find largely null or near-null estimates. For voter registration, modest effects are confined to certain minority groups and counties with high concentrations of Democratic voters, foreign-born residents, or Hispanic populations, while for other groups and places, effects are statistically indistinguishable from zero. For donations, we detect substantively negligible effects—equivalent to roughly one additional donor contributing ≈$200 per county, present only on the day of arrest. Moreover, these effects are offsetting, positive for both Democrats and Republicans, and somewhat sensitive to violations of parallel trends. Our findings hold across a battery of explanations for the null and survive a demanding critical-case test using ICE activity in Minnesota in late 2025 and early 2026. Even where ICE activity generated substantial attention, effects are indistinguishable from zero. Together, our results suggest that even historically unprecedented immigration enforcement, in even the most salient local contexts, does not automatically translate into political mobilization. These findings contribute to the contested literature on the political consequences of the state’s exercise of punitive power.

articles.title: Marcos teóricos para democratizar el conocimiento científico y sus implicaciones en la salud pública
articles.abstract: Este ensayo ofrece un panorama de los principales abordajes teóricos orientados a la democratización de la ciencia: a) la nueva producción del conocimiento, b) la ciencia posnormal y c) la ciencia ciudadana; asimismo, se analizan las implicaciones de estos enfoques para la salud pública. En todos ellos se observa un reconocimiento explícito de la incertidumbre, con un mayor énfasis y desarrollo teórico en la ciencia posnormal. Los tres enfoques promueven procesos de participación que permiten a la ciudadanía comprender tanto las fortalezas como las limitaciones del conocimiento científico. En el caso de la ciencia ciudadana, se identifican posturas diversas que abogan por un mayor grado de participación e involucramiento de la ciudadanía en la toma de decisiones. La ciencia posnormal enfatiza que, pese a la incertidumbre, existen circunstancias en las que resulta urgente implementar acciones debido a lo que está en juego; este enfoque teórico reconoce, además, la relevancia del conocimiento no científico y de una comunidad ampliada de revisores para legitimar la implementación de intervenciones. Por su parte, la nueva producción del conocimiento plantea la necesidad de interactuar con la sociedad privilegiando el conocimiento científico, lo que supone un desafío de legitimidad en el ámbito de la salud pública, debido a que podría favorecer una participación inequitativa, a expensas de los sectores ciudadanos con menores niveles educativos.

articles.title: THE DISPLACEMENT HYPOTHESIS Does Suppression of Consensual Adult Sexual Activity Correlate with Increased Child Sexual Victimisation? A Comparative Empirical Analysis
articles.abstract: Child sexual abuse remains among the most egregious manifestations of predatory human behaviour, yet its structural determinants are seldom examined through the lens of regulatory failure rather than individual pathology alone. This paper advances the Displacement Hypothesis: when legitimate, consensual, and regulated expressions of adult sexuality are systematically suppressed — through criminalisation, social stigma, or institutional prohibition — the resulting demand doesn’t extinguish itself; rather, it displaces toward victims who are most structurally vulnerable, including children and non-consenting adults. Drawing on comparative empirical data from six jurisdictions — the Netherlands, New Zealand, Rhode Island (USA), Sweden, Germany, and India — this paper examines documented correlations between the regulatory architecture governing consensual adult sexual activity and the incidence of child sexual victimisation and coercive sexual crime. The analysis is descriptive and comparative; it makes no normative prescription regarding any particular regulatory model, but identifies a significant research gap warranting systematic empirical investigation in the Indian context. The paper integrates three significant bodies of contemporary evidence: the global #MeToo movement (2017–present) as a structural indicator of the pervasive institutional tolerance of sexual harm; the Supreme Court of India's progressive jurisprudence on sex worker dignity in Buddhadev Karmaskar v. State of West Bengal (2011, 2022); and the comparative regulatory evidence from decriminalised jurisdictions. The paper situates this analysis within the broader Regulatory Realism framework developed across the author's prior published works, arguing that any comprehensive primary prevention strategy for child sexual abuse — including the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model proposed in Gupta (2026a) — must account for the structural demand-side dynamics that current Indian law leaves unaddressed.

articles.title: Buzz as an Urban Resource
articles.abstract: The global rise of arts and culture is transforming local politics. Though new to many academic urban analysts, this is a commonplace for many mayors and local policy makers around the world. We seek to overcome this divide by joining culture and the arts with classic concepts of urban politics. We offer an analytical framework incorporating the politics of cultural policy alongside the typical political economic concerns in the urban politics and development lit- eratures. Our framework synthesizes several research streams that combine in global factors driving the articulation of culture into city politics. This frames our studies of the local processes through which this articulation occurs on the ground in Toronto.

articles.title: Romantic Partner Search: The Endogenous-Horizon Optimal Stopping Problem with Somatic Maintenance Trade-offs
articles.abstract: Loneliness is a population-scale mortality risk, and its time-cost bites especially hard for adults who hope to build a family under a biological deadline. Individuals pursuing aggressive somatic optimization face a time-allocation trade-off that can be formalized through sequential search mathematics. Every hour spent on somatic maintenance, whether sleep protocols, training, nutrition, or longevity biohacking, extends the search horizon for a romantic partner and typically raises mate value. Every same hour is an hour not spent meeting people, so the rate at which new candidates arrive falls in direct proportion to the time spent in isolation. The body being optimized is useless to the project without someone to share it with. We formalize this trade-off by joining optimal stopping theory with life-history biology. Three results follow. First, the optimal allocation is bounded strictly below the full daily budget: even at arbitrarily high intervention efficacy (the "longevity escape velocity" limit), a rational searcher devotes no more than 50% of daily bandwidth to somatic maintenance in the symmetric-linear case, and less under diminishing biological returns. Beyond that line, isolation destroys more romantic value than infinite time can ever recover. Second, the asymmetric clock of female fecundity forces reservation thresholds to fall on a steep Riccati curve. Fertility preservation then becomes a high-stakes wager: it pays off when deployed early and with high clinic efficacy, and it backfires when deployed late or on low-efficacy interventions. Third, the model's abstract arrival rate λ maps onto an empirical, measurable construct, the Social Connectivity Value (SCV) framework (Schumacher, 2026), which specifies the weekly social floor that must remain protected and the corresponding cap on somatic investment. The prescription is precise: invest seriously in health, cap that investment at the (k−1)/(2k) ceiling derived below, and protect a weekly minimum of real-world social exposure as non-negotiable infrastructure. Longevity must serve the goal of flourishing, not become a sterile substitute for it.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Estimation of exact months for the microdata and rolling quarter series from PNAD Contínua
articles.authors: Marcos Dantas Hecksher, Rogério J. Barbosa
articles.abstract: Brazil’s Continuous National Household Sample Survey (PNADC) publishes labor market indicators for rolling quarters, concealing the precise timing and magnitude of economic shocks. This article presents two complementary methods for recovering genuine monthly estimates. The microdata approach exploits the rotating panel design and respondent birthdates to deterministically identify the exact reference month of each interview, achieving a 97% determination rate across 28.4 million observations (2012–2025) with monthly samples 51% larger than those of the old Monthly Employment Survey (PME). The aggregate approach inverts the rolling-quarter averaging formula for over 80 official indicators published by the Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics (IBGE). Validation uses minimum wage transitions: the predictable one-month lag between habitual and effective income confirms identification accuracy across 16 adjustments. Applications show that COVID-19 peak unemployment was 2 percentage points higher than quarterly data indicate, that monthly poverty captures the precise Auxílio Emergencial effect, and that weekly data reveal differentiated carnival impacts across metropolitan regions. Both methods are implemented in an open-source R package.

articles.title: Do LLMs Read for Free? Auditing the Open Access Bias in AI-Mediated Science
articles.abstract: How large language models affect various social activities has been widely discussed; however, how large language models themselves influence the mechanisms of scientific knowledge production remains unclear. To investigate this question, this paper designs a natural experiment based on a large-scale bibliometric dataset. Using a Difference-in-Differences model with Poisson Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood estimation and analyzing citation records from 64,841 articles across 120 communication journals, I find that the emergence of large language models amplifies the visibility gap between Open Access (OA) and non-OA articles. Specifically, OA articles experienced a significant post-ChatGPT citation acceleration of approximately 0.84% per month, accumulating to roughly 10% over 12 months. Importantly, this estimate represents a conservative lower bound, as gradual LLM adoption and journal review lags attenuate the measured treatment intensity. The findings suggest that while large language models promote knowledge dissemination, they also exacerbate inequality in academic knowledge production.

articles.title: HCBfT: A High-Cognitive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Turkish
articles.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) approach human-level performance on traditional English-centric benchmarks, evaluating their capabilities in morphologically rich and resource-poor languages has become a critical challenge. Existing Turkish benchmarks frequently rely on direct translations of English datasets or basic arithmetic tasks stripped of semantic context, failing to capture true linguistic and cultural comprehension. We introduce the High-Cognitive Benchmark for Turkish (HCBfT) and its inaugural version, TR200. This private dataset comprises 200 multiple-choice adversarial questions. To construct it, we combined original items with questions distilled from an initial pool of more than 300,000 standardized test questions. Eliminating redundant and low-difficulty items provided an agile, cost-efficient and highly discriminative evaluation framework. HCBfT-TR200 evaluates advanced native-level comprehension, complex verbal logic, non-standard dialects, cultural alignment and multidisciplinary domain knowledge specific to Turkey. We keep the dataset strictly private to ensure contamination-free evaluation. The original questions resist simple information retrieval. We filtered all candidate items through a diverse panel of frontier LLMs to identify the most challenging subsets (where more than 75% of models failed) and augmented them with human-authored adversarial traps. We evaluated 20 mid-sized and frontier models (10 API, 10 local). A general educated adult human baseline, established through a controlled administration to 24 native Turkish-speaking undergraduates under an anchored incomplete block design, yielded 37.9% accuracy. Results demonstrate that HCBfT-TR200 presents a significant challenge to LLMs. The commercial leader, Gemini 3 Flash, achieved 68.0% accuracy. Mistral Large 3, the open-weight model by Paris-based Mistral AI, ranked second at 52.5% while costing $0.07 per correct answer. The top local open-weight model, Gemma 4 31B IT, achieved 42.0%. Ministral 3 14B Reasoning led in hardware efficiency with a ratio of 6.7 correct answers per gigabyte (CPG). By providing a standardized metric for open-weight and API models, HCBfT serves as a reliable guide for the secure and efficient deployment of culturally aligned, locally hosted AI systems in the Turkish public and private sectors. Future iterations of the benchmark will broaden this framework beyond multiple-choice evaluation to rigorously assess open-ended generation, instruction following and culturally context-aware persona adoption.

articles.title: Embracing the Apocalypse: Existential Spoilers and Risk Mitigation
articles.abstract: Existential risk assessment, planning, and mitigation need to account for the potential for actors to spoil planned or implemented risk reduction measures. For example, actors might sabotage negotiations between warring nuclear powers, or disrupt global climate change cooperation. The article describes and analyzes the different types, motivations, and risk dynamics of existential spoilers. The harms from an existential spoiler can range broadly from virtually guaranteeing existential harm manifests to eliminating an existential risk scenario entirely. Existential spoilers may be motivated by ambivalence / ignorance, lack of capacity, a belief the mitigation is not necessary, a belief the mitigation is harmful, or that existential harm is good. Existential risk mitigation measures vary in their susceptibility to three types of existential spoiler: non-cooperative, physical, or systemic spoilers. As existential risks are global in nature, protecting existential risk reduction measures is a new potential avenue for global security cooperation.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Causal Carbon: Baselines and Additionality with Potential Outcomes
articles.authors: Megan Ayers, Luke Sanford, Will Gardner, Sara Kuebbing
articles.abstract: Recent work has questioned the credibility of forest carbon offsets as a climate solution. This threatens both investor confidence and genuine climate mitigation efforts in the voluntary carbon market, which has contracted by over 75% since 2021. Despite updated methodologies and widespread advice to invest only in high-integrity or high-quality credits, it remains unclear which credits genuinely meet these criteria. Here, we draw on the fields of statistics and causal inference to develop a generalized analytical framework for evaluating the additionality of credits generated by carbon offset protocols, addressing persistent ambiguities and limitations in current approaches. This framework comes from a systematic evaluation of all existing forest carbon offset protocols. We translated each protocol's baseline methodology into a statistical causal estimator and derived the assumptions necessary for it to produce accurate estimates of generated credits. By translating those assumptions back into the language of forest carbon credits we provide a set of conditions that buyers should believe in order to conclude that the credits they purchase are additional. We demonstrate that strategic enrollment, combined with even minor measurement errors in carbon stocks, can lead to significant market distortions, undermining both environmental integrity and financial reliability of offsets. Our analysis highlights an inherent tradeoff between ensuring accurate carbon measurement and expanding participation in offset programs. This framework clarifies the assumptions purchasers of carbon offsets must accept for credits under each protocol to reliably represent genuine climate impacts, providing a transparent basis for buyers, sellers, critics, and advocates to constructively engage and reconcile divergent views.

articles.title: Internal Dialogue During Divergent Thinking Predicts Originality—But in Unexpected Ways
articles.abstract: Internal dialogue may serve a key self-regulatory function in creative thinking. This study extends previous research by contrasting the contributions of trait-level internal dialogue—reflecting general tendencies to engage in dialogical activity—and state-level internal dialogue, reported retrospectively after completing divergent thinking tasks. Three hundred fifty adults were recruited online and completed two Alternate Uses Tests. Originality was assessed using both a uniqueness-based method and a validated artificial intelligence scoring technique. After accounting for individual differences in openness to experience, trait-level internal dialogue showed an inconsistent association with originality. In contrast, state-level internal dialogue consistently predicted originality, demonstrating predictive power beyond trait-level dialogue and openness to experience. Contrary to our hypothesis, however, this association was negative. Higher levels of internal dialogue during the creative task were related to lower originality. This unexpected pattern suggests that internal dialogue may not always support ideation. Alternative explanations for these findings, along with discrepancies with prior research, are examined to guide future studies into the potential role of internal dialogue in creative thinking.

articles.title: Does Democratization Reduce Inequality? A Critical Appraisal of the Middle-Ground Theory
articles.abstract: Arguing against prior theories that democratization has no impact on income inequality, Dorsch and Maarek (2019) contend in an APSR article that democratization causes extreme income distributions to move towards a "middle ground," reducing inequality in highly unequal autocracies while increasing inequality in relatively egalitarian ones. Central to the study’s evidence is an instrumental variable strategy that leverages the regional share of democracies, and its interaction with initial inequality levels, to identify both the effect of democratization and the democracy–inequality interaction. We provide a critical replication of this study, making two central contributions. First, we show that the generated instrument violates the exclusion restriction and, second, even when properly constructed, the same linear function cannot be used to identify two causal effects. We then demonstrate how the same source of exogenous variation can be used to identify multiple causal effects using a generalized additive model, with non-linearities in the first stages serving as additional instruments. Across all specifications, the data do not support the middle‑ground theory proposed by the authors: neither democracy nor its interaction with initial inequality has a statistically significant effect on the Gini coefficient. Our findings are consistent with an extensive literature in economics and political science that has struggled to uncover a systematic democracy–inequality link. The replication method we employ offers a practical tool for other studies in contexts where valid instruments are scarce or the exclusion restriction is difficult to satisfy.

articles.title: Energy Bills and Manufactured Climate Backlash
articles.abstract: Can populist radical right (PRR) parties mobilize green backlash when climate policy did not cause the costs voters face? I argue backlash can arise without direct policy costs if PRR parties supply a causal account linking external shocks to climate policy. I examine the UK's 2021–2023 energy price shock, driven by global fossil fuel markets rather than domestic policy. Analyzing party press releases and YouTube transcripts, I find UKIP and Reform UK repeatedly tied higher bills to Net Zero. Leveraging pre-shock geographic variation in housing energy efficiency from 27 million energy performance certificates, I show individuals in less efficient areas became more likely to support PRR parties after 2021, with effects concentrated among gas-dependent households. Survey evidence shows exposed respondents became more likely to say environmental protections had "gone too far." Findings demonstrate that climate commitments are politically vulnerable beyond their material reach.

articles.title: The Hidden Costs of Gatekeeping in Open Science: Quasi-Experimental Evidence from arXiv's Endorsement Policy Tightening
articles.abstract: Open science platforms promise to democratize scholarly communication, yet many impose social network-dependent barriers to entry. We study the causal effect of arXiv's January 2026 endorsement policy tightening---which required new submitters to secure endorsement from established authors rather than relying on institutional affiliation alone---on new author entry rates. Constructing a panel of 143 subcategories observed weekly over 94 weeks from 3 million papers, we estimate Poisson quasi-maximum likelihood models with two-way fixed effects. The policy caused a 13--18% overall decline in new submitter rates, with catastrophic effects on disciplines with sparse co-authorship networks: economics (-49%) and quantitative biology (-49%) lost nearly half their new entrants, while well-connected fields like physics were unaffected. A formal interaction model confirms that pre-treatment co-authorship network density significantly buffers the policy shock (p = .005). Event study, staggered difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and a bioRxiv placebo test support causality. These findings reveal that access policies conditioning participation on pre-existing social networks systematically exclude researchers from peripheral communities, reinforcing the very inequalities open science purports to dismantle.

articles.title: “It is a soothing sensory experience” but “I also find it overwhelming”: A mixed-methods study of Autistic adults’ sensory experiences engaging with nature
articles.authors: Eleanor Smith, Samantha Friedman, Keren MacLennan
articles.abstract: Autistic people often experience sensory overload in many everyday environments, but nature spaces, such as parks and woodlands, provide a more positive sensory experience for some. Still, nature can pose challenges, and little research has focused on Autistic adults’ sensory experiences in these settings. We conducted the first study exploring this topic, using an online mixed-methods survey of 113 Autistic adults. Quantitative analysis revealed that most participants engaged with nature by spending time outdoors, typically 1–5 hours per week, and were generally satisfied with this level of engagement. However, those with higher sensory hyperresponsivity and sensory seeking tendencies reported natural spaces as more disabling. Qualitative analysis of 107 responses revealed three key themes: (1) nature as a source of sensory regulation and restoration, (2) nature as an escape from overwhelming people and environments, and (3) moments when sensory challenges disrupted the connection with nature. Overall, many Autistic adults find nature restorative and supportive but also face barriers that can diminish its benefits or cause distress. These findings highlight the importance of designing inclusive outdoor spaces and programs that account for sensory needs. Supporting Autistic adults’ access to nature can promote well-being, inclusion, and meaningful connection with the environment.

articles.title: The Worth of Nature: Ascertaining the Effect of Ecosystem Services on Property Value Formation in Chile
articles.authors: Ernesto Lopez-Morales, Luis Inostroza, Nicolas Herrera, Ana Luisa Araos, Vicente Mosso
articles.abstract: This study estimates the impact of ecosystem services (ES) on rural land values in southern Chile using a hedonic pricing model. The methodology integrates over 80,000 georeferenced rustic plot transactions (1999–2023) with expert-scored data on 32 ES, including 21 regulating and 11 cultural services, mapped onto a hexagonal tessellation of structural land types. Spatial proximity to ES sources was weighted using inverse-distance functions. The results show significant and diverse effects of ES on land prices, revealing both positive (e.g., waste filtering) and negative (e.g., pollination) valuation impacts. These findings reveal critical gaps in Chile’s current tax appraisal system, which overlooks the contribution of natural capital to the formation of land value. The study emphasizes the need to modernize property taxation by incorporating the various contributions of ecosystem services to land. This approach enhances redistributive potential and strengthens territorial equity, particularly in ecologically sensitive, rapidly subdividing rural areas.

articles.title: Gaining Ground: Minimum Wage and Gender Wage Gap in Mexico, 2015–2025
articles.abstract: This study examines the impact of the doubling of the real minimum wage in Mexico (2015–2025) on the gender wage gap. While labor market discrimination remains pervasive, the causal drivers of gap reduction are under-researched. Utilizing data from INEGI’s Employment Survey (ENOE) and OLS regression analysis, this research segments the labor market by wage level, contract type, and job position to identify heterogeneous effects. The findings provide strong evidence for the lighthouse effect, demonstrating that minimum wage hikes serve as a bottom-up equalizer. The gender gap in the low-wage bracket plummeted from 9% to 0%, with significant reductions also observed among temporary and non-contractual workers. This suggests the minimum wage functions as a powerful social benchmark beyond the regulated sector. However, the policy proved less effective in middle- and high-income clerical and managerial roles. The study concludes that while aggressive minimum wage policies effectively mitigate the sticky floor, targeted sector-specific interventions remain necessary to achieve full gender pay equity.

articles.title: The Early Interplay of Childcare Histories and Maternal Employment Trajectories: A Multichannel Sequence Analysis
articles.abstract: Objective: This study examines the joint development of childcare and maternal employment trajectories and their social structure. Background: Early childhood represents a critical period when families organize care and employment under institutional constraints and normative expectations. Decisions about maternal employment and childcare are interconnected and shape both gender inequalities in the labour market and children’s early life conditions. Capturing how these arrangements evolve requires a trajectory-based perspective, considering their interrelated dynamic development across early family life courses. Method: We use linked survey and administrative data (NEPS-ADIAB) on n=1521 mother-child dyads in Germany from register-based sampling, providing full panel details on care and employment. Multichannel sequence analysis models monthly trajectories across both domains until age five. Multinomial regressions assess how socio-demographic, attitudinal, and contextual factors are associated with cluster affiliation. Results: Six childcare-work clusters emerged, categorized into two overarching patterns: family-focused patterns show low workforce and family-dominant care, whereas work-centred patterns reveal intense employment and early institutional care. Education and preferences emerge as key differentiator, with minor relevance of contextual factors. Conclusion: Findings highlight that reconciling childcare and employment is shaped by multiple, socially stratified pathways into diverse arrangements, rather than a unified strategy, as similar childcare strategies do not translate into similar employment patterns, and vice versa. Analysing work and childcare as interdependent trajectories reveals inequalities in childcare-wok-patterns that remain obscured in previous transitional approaches.

articles.title: Issue Competition on Social Media: A new comparative dataset on Parties' issue agendas
articles.abstract: Understanding how parties compete by shaping agendas and promoting issue priorities is central to the study of party competition. Existing research relies heavily on manifestos, limiting analyses to election periods and offline contexts, while newer datasets lack cross-national breadth, temporal depth, and conceptual harmonization. We introduce a comparative dataset of party issue agendas derived from 4,600,000 social media posts for 219 parties across 18 European countries on Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram. Using supervised text classification aligned with the Comparative Agendas Project, we generate monthly measures of salience over more than a decade, providing conceptually standardized, cross-platform, and cross-nationally comparable indicators of issue attention. We validate our measures through comparisons over time, across party families, major political shocks, and existing databases, and illustrate the dataset's leverage by showing how mainstream parties' immigration attention is associated with radical-right support gains. This dataset substantially expands the empirical infrastructure for studying dynamic issue competition.

articles.title: The Paper Factory
articles.abstract: How can large language models (LLMs) contribute to social science research, and what parts of research remain stubbornly human? Building on existing LLM tools, we offer a multi-agent workflow capable of producing a full quantitative social science paper from an initial prompt. The workflow relies on researchers codifying their heuristics for doing data analysis, and we suggest some core design principles for researchers interested in building on this scaffolding. Using this case, we also examine what current LLM capabilities reveal about the organization of research. LLM agents can lower the cost of pursuing high-risk ideas, expand robustness and transparency, bring research to new audiences, and force scholars to articulate the heuristics that create valuable research. But they also pose challenges, both in terms of the quality of papers and in the adequacy of scientific institutions to adapt. Meeting these challenges will require not prohibition or denial, but new institutional norms that make use of these tools observable, auditable, and accountable.

articles.title: Rethinking Maritime Origins: Ecological Rhythms and Maritime Network Formation Before Trade
articles.authors: Veronica Walker Vadillo, Wesa Perttola, Marko Kallio, Victoria Ramenzoni
articles.abstract: Maritime networks have traditionally been explained through the lens of trade, exchange, and state expansion. This article argues that such approaches mistake mature functions for generative causes. Instead, it proposes a relational framework in which maritime connectivity emerges from repeated engagement with spatially and temporally structured aquatic resources. To capture this process, we introduce the concept of Repetitive Ecological Mobility (REM), defined as the structured repetition of movement in response to ecological rhythms, through which stable and recognizable patterns of connectivity emerge. These engagements—particularly in contexts of seasonal abundance, ecological patchiness, and risk—produce navigational knowledge, temporal coordination, provisioning systems, and nodal aggregation. Over time, these relations become progressively stabilized through processes of adjustment and alignment (i.e. dynamic stability), constituting the conditions under which maritime networks become durable, scalable, and available for repurposing, including trade. Maritime networks are therefore best understood not as commerce-driven systems, but as historically layered socio-ecological formations rooted in livelihood practices.

articles.title: Measuring Divergence and Convergence in Sequence Analysis: A Spell-Based Extension of Longest Common Prefixes
articles.abstract: Measuring sequence dissimilarity is central to sequence analysis, yet empirical applications remain dominated by optimal matching (OM), a widely used but contested approach. Longest common prefix (LCP) measures offer an alternative by capturing shared beginnings and endings, making them useful for studying divergence and convergence, important concepts in social science. However, existing LCP variants are position-wise: small shifts in transition timing can break the common prefix between trajectories that share the same spell sequence, making them appear to diverge early. This study develops a spell-based perspective on understanding sequence comparison. We introduce OMspellUnitFree (a modified spell-based OM) and propose LCPspell, a spell-based extension of LCP. Using simulations and an empirical demonstration, we show that whole-trajectory measures such as OM family capture broad trajectory profiles, whereas LCP-type measures identify where similarity breaks down or reappears. The paper provides practical guidance for choosing distance measures according to research questions and data characteristics.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: MEDLEX CONTEXT: ISOLATED VS CONTEXTUALISED LEXICAL PROCESSING IN MEDICAL ENGLISH LISTENING
articles.abstract: This study examines isolated vs contextualised lexical presentation in Medical English listening within an English for Specific Purposes (ESP) framework, extending the MedLex Context paradigm from reading to auditory processing. It investigates whether contextual embedding facilitates receptive lexical access under listening conditions and how effects differ from reading-based findings.Using a counterbalanced within-item design, 40 second-year medical students completed two listening tasks based on the same corpus-derived set of 30 lexical items: an isolated word recognition task and a contextualised sentence-based inference task. Audio materials were delivered under controlled timing with repeated exposure.Results show no uniform group-level advantage, although contextualised listening produced a small but statistically significant improvement in accuracy (p = .025). However, item-level analysis reveals strong variability, with both contextual facilitation and contextual interference. Some lexical items benefited from sentence-level cues, while others showed reduced accuracy due to increased processing demands.Findings suggest that contextualisation does not simply support lexical access but redistributes cognitive load across simultaneous processes in ESP listening, where auditory input is transient and heavily reliant on working memory. Compared to reading, listening shows a more balanced but highly variable interaction between context and lexical accessibility.The study contributes to a modality-sensitive account of vocabulary processing in ESP and supports staged instruction integrating isolated and contextualised listening tasks.

articles.title: MEDLEX CONTEXT: ISOLATED VS CONTEXTUALISED LEXICAL PROCESSING IN MEDICAL ENGLISH READING
articles.abstract: This study examines the effects of isolated and contextualised lexical presentation on reading comprehension in Medical English within an English for Specific Purposes (ESP) framework. Cognitive accounts of language processing suggest that contextualisation does not inherently reduce difficulty but instead redistributes cognitive load across multiple processing systems [1][2]. Corpus-based perspectives further indicate that lexical meaning in specialised discourse emerges through patterned usage rather than isolated occurrence [3]. Using a counterbalanced within-item design, 40 second-year medical students completed two tasks based on a corpus-derived set of 30 lexical items, involving isolated form–meaning recognition and contextualised sentence-based inference. The results reveal a statistically significant advantage for isolated presentation, reflected in higher mean accuracy and more stable performance across groups. Inferential analysis confirms that direct form–meaning mapping provides a more efficient pathway to lexical access in reading. At the same time, item-level analysis demonstrates substantial variability, with both facilitative and inhibitory effects of context.Within the MedLex framework, vocabulary knowledge is conceptualised as a dynamic system shaped by lexical properties, input structure, and learner resources [4][5]. The findings support a staged pedagogical approach in which isolated lexical work provides a stable foundation, followed by progressively more demanding contextualised tasks aligned with learner readiness.

articles.title: MEDLEX25: DIAGNOSING THE RECOGNITION–PRODUCTION GAP IN MEDICAL ENGLISH VOCABULARY
articles.abstract: This study introduces MedLex-25, a compact diagnostic instrument designed to quantify recognition–production asymmetries in medical English vocabulary. Second-year medical students at Medical University Varna, Bulgaria completed nine parallel test forms (50 targets per form; 225 unique lexical targets sampled from a coursebook corpus) under supervised conditions; usable data from 186 students were analysed, with a total of 9300 questions answered. Each form paired a four-option receptive item with a sentence-production task for the same targets; production responses were binary-scored (correct/incorrect) with tolerance for non-meaning-preventing surface errors. Results suggest a systematic recognition–production gap across five lexical categories (nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, two-word multiword items): receptive accuracy consistently exceeded productive accuracy, and no item achieved perfect production. Counts of perfectly recognized items were: nouns 33/45, adjectives 27/45, verbs 23/45, adverbs 25/45, and multiword items 28/45. Verbs, adjectives and—most strikingly—adverbs exhibited the largest productive weaknesses (lowest production for erroneously = 0.071). Item-level diagnostics identify concrete teaching targets. The MedLex-25 test can guide focused instruction and recommend follow-up studies that expand item coverage, refine production scoring, and investigate etymology and cross-disciplinary exposure as mediating factors.

articles.title: METACOGNITIVE MISMATCH IN MEDICAL ESP: EXPLORING THE RECOGNITION–PRODUCTION GAP
articles.abstract: This study examines the recognition–production gap (a.k.a. metacognitive mismatch) in Medical English for Specific Purposes (ESP). Two paired tasks — a receptive matching/definition task and a brief production task (write an original sentence) — were administered to 214 medical students using 10 target medical terms. Results show very high receptive knowledge but a notable decline in productive use. Depending on the aggregation used, receptive accuracy was 2084/2140 (97.38%) while productive accuracy was either 1706/2140 (79.72%). The mean per-student scores were 9.74/10 (comprehension) and 7.97/10 (production, aggregate). The average recognition–production gap is approximately 17.7–18.6 percentage points. Error patterns in production indicate frequent near-misses (morphosyntactic or collocational errors) rather than pure semantic gaps. Pedagogical recommendations include focused production drills, collocation practice, and targeted feedback on common error types.

articles.title: Related diversification, not generic breadth: industrial composition and urban resilience to the COVID-19 shock
articles.abstract: Industrial diversity is widely held to buffer urban economies against economic shocks, yet evidence remains mixed and the operative mechanisms poorly understood. Using establishment-count data for 131 wards across nine Japanese designated cities and Tokyo (2016--2021), we apply Bayesian spatial error models to examine how three diversity dimensions---within-ward breadth ($\alpha$-diversity), between-ward compositional heterogeneity ($\beta$-diversity), and related/unrelated variety---independently predict ward-level resilience during the COVID-19 shock. Related diversity is robustly protective while unrelated diversity is harmful---a sign reversal relative to long-run growth studies that we attribute to the sector-discriminating character of the pandemic: cross-sector portfolio breadth provides no hedging mechanism when proximity-dependent industries collapse simultaneously. Shannon entropy's positive association with resilience masks this opposition between related and unrelated variety. $\alpha$-diversity and sectoral concentration (HHI) exhibit mutual suppression, implying that industrial breadth confers resilience specifically when organised around an anchor sector, while $\beta$-diversity proves to reflect spatial clustering of the information and communications technology sector rather than general compositional heterogeneity. These results imply that the policy-relevant target is strategic \emph{related} diversification---broadening within-sector-family portfolios---rather than maximising generic industrial breadth, and that the operative diversity mechanism depends critically on the type of shock a city faces.

articles.title: Stimmenvielfalt oder Gruppenbildung? Der Jaccard-Index zur quantitativen Analyse von Akteur:innenkopplungen in journalistischen Texten
articles.authors: Tabea Lüders, Viviane Seeberger, Markus Lehmkuhl
articles.abstract: Die Untersuchung von Akteur:innenstrukturen in medial vermittelten öffentlichen Diskursen ist ein Anliegen zahlreicher Inhaltsanalysen. Nur in wenigen Fällen werden diese akteur:innenzentrierten Erhebungen jedoch genutzt, um Aussagen, die über die Beschreibung bestimmter Merkmalsverteilungen hinausgehen, zu treffen. Diskurstheorien legen jedoch nahe, dass öffentliche Diskurse nicht nur durch die bloße Präsenz verschiedener Akteur:innen entstehen, sondern erst dann, wenn zwischen ihnen ein (kontroverser) Austausch entsteht. Interaktionen und Beziehungen zwischen Akteur:innen sind somit ebenso zentral für die strukturelle Beschreibung öffentlicher Diskurse wie die Akteur:innen selbst. Die systematische Erfassung und Analyse solcher relationalen Variablen ist allerdings aufwendig, kompliziert und bislang auch an wenige standardisierte Verfahren und vergleichbare Metriken geknüpft. In unserem Paper zeigen wir daher zunächst anhand verschiedener Beispielstudien auf, wie Akteur:innenbeziehungen bereits untersucht wurden und schlagen anschließend ein weiteres Verfahren vor, das den in der Computerlinguistik verbreiteten Jaccard-Index nutzbar macht, um heterogene Ko-Präsenzen von Akteur:innen in der medialen Berichterstattung sichtbar zu machen. Dieses Verfahren wird an den Beispielen zitierter Akteur:innen in der deutschen Berichterstattung über den Klimawandel, Künstliche Intelligenz und Neurowissenschaften exemplifiziert.

articles.title: Does Governance Affect Economic Growth? Evidence from SANE Countries
articles.authors: Uchechukwu Innocent Duru, Omoyokan Paul Ehidiamhen, Adanne Millicent Eze, Chikeziem Fortunatus Okorontah, Emmanuel Ejike Odeh, Kelechi Promise Uzoma, Toluwalashe Favour Ojo, Chukwuka Christian Ekechi
articles.abstract: This study examined the link between the six World Bank World Governance Indicators of voice and accountability, political stability and absence of violence, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, and control of corruption and economic growth in SANE countries: South Africa, Algeria, Nigeria and Egypt from 1996 to 2023. Due to the multicollinearity brought on by a strong connection between some pairings of the governance indices, the unique determinants of governance from among the six dimensions of governance were chosen using the principal component analysis. Control of corruption was the only factor with an eigenvalue larger than one and accounted for over 74% of the variability in the initial data after the principal component analysis. It was termed the index of good governance. The relationship between the index of good governance and economic growth was studied in both the short and long-term using the Pooled Mean Group Panel Autoregressive Distributed Lag approach. The findings showed that the index of good governance had a negative and insignificant impact on economic growth. However, foreign direct investment and domestic credit to the private sector had a positive effect on economic growth. The study advises governments and policymakers in the SANE economies to sustain existing policies on foreign direct investment since it is yielding the desired outcome and foster an atmosphere that draws foreign direct investment. Policies for guaranteeing the growth of the private sector through domestic financing should also be sustained.

articles.title: Predicted Repayment Behaviour of Borrowers in Urban Cooperative Banks in Assam: A Case Study of Nagarik Samabay Bank
articles.abstract: The purpose of this study is to analyse the predicted repayment behavior of sample borrowers of Nagarik Samabay Bank, an Urban Cooperative Bank in Assam for the period 2023-24 by employing the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model. The results indicate that eleven socio-economic factors were statistically significant in predicting the repayment behavior of sample borrowers, with the ANN model achieving a predicted default classification accuracy of 83.3%. However, in comparison, the logistic regression (LR) model demonstrated higher predictive performance, correctly classifying defaulters with an accuracy of 88.2%, thereby outperforming the ANN model. However, the ANN model predicted regular borrowers more accurately than logistic regression, achieving an accuracy of 98.9% compared to 97.6%. Based on these findings, it is suggested that financial institutions, particularly Urban Cooperative Banks, adopt robust risk assessment measures to strengthen their position in the banking sector.
